details.text: Derivative: الرَّحْمَٰنُ – The word “mercy” is not the best way to depict the meaning of “Rahmah” (رَحْمَة) in the English language from which the attribute of Allah الرَّحْمَٰنُ (Ar-Rahman) is derived. In English literature it is understood as a lack, forgoing or sparing of punishment, where someone shows mercy by not punishing or killing someone else for example. Mercy in the English language is used in relation to something bad not happening rather than being something good in origin. “Rahmah” (رَحْمَة) comes from the رَحِم (Rahim) which is the womb of the mother. It relates to being taken care of from every single angle. As such, الرَّحْمَٰنُ (Ar-Rahman) implies the One who takes care of us in every single way and of all our concerns.
Derivative: أَنَامِ is the word used by Allah to describe all living creatures.

Derivative: فَاكِهَةٌ – ثَمَر (Thamar) in Arabic is the general word for fruits. The verb فِكِهَ means to be happy. Therefore, فَاكِهَةٌ implies the type of fruit that makes you happy, brings joy to you and makes you smile.
Derivative: الْأَكْمَامِ are sheaths or sleeves which contain dates.
Derivative: Plants that emit sweet smelling aromas and fragrances in the air carried by breezes and the wind.
Derivative: آلَاءِ (Aalaa’) is the plural of إلًى (Ilan) and refers to amazing favours, miracles, wonders, blessings etc.
Derivative: صَلْصَالٍ refers to dried up clay.
Derivative: مَارِجٍ is the invisible and flameless part of fire that is free of smoke. It’s fused with the flame but cannot be seen.

Derivative: الْقُرْآنَ is in the Mubaalaghah form (صِيغَةُ المُبَالَغَة). It is hyperbole which means a manner of empowering a word and emphasising it. قِراءَةٌ (Qiraa’ah) means to read and الْقُرْآنَ (the Qur’an) means to read a lot. This form can provide the implication that الْقُرْآنَ (the Qur’an) represents reading itself in the sense that He (الرَّحْمَٰنُ) taught reading itself and also as the الْقُرْآنَ (the Qur’an) is the name of the Book, some linguists have combined the two meanings to state that Allah is saying that nobody has experienced reading itself until they have read الْقُرْآنَ (the Qur’an) i.e., the pinnacle of reading is الْقُرْآنَ (the Qur’an). In addition, the آنَ at the end of الْقُرْآنَ signifies repetition or extremeness which means that you can’t learn this Book until you have done a lot of reading and rereading and rereading and so on.

Derivative: عَلَّمَ – يُعَلِّمُ – تَعْلِيم means to deliver knowledge over time, little by little. The تَفْعِيل pattern suggests something that takes some time. Taking the time and patience to teach is implied inherently in its meaning.

عَلَّمَ الْقُرْآنَ

- This is the second part of the sentence, the Khabar (predicate), in a Jumlah Ismiyah (noun sentence) where the first part of the sentence, the Mubtada’, is the first verse preceding it: “الرَّحْمَٰنُ”. The purpose of the use of a Jumlah Ismiyah (noun sentence) is to reaffirm and establish the presence of someone other than the subject. It’s used therefore affects the translation, so instead of stating “The Incredibly Merciful taught the Qur’an”, it means: “The Incredibly merciful, in fact, He taught the Qur’an” and implying “not anybody else”. الرَّحْمَٰنُ is the One who taught the Qur’an and anybody else who teaches it is the means for teaching what He (الرَّحْمَٰنُ) taught.
Derivative: الْمُنْشَآتُ is the plural of مُنْشَأَة and it is an اِسْم مَفْعُول (Ism Maf’ool). It refers to something that has been constructed to an elevated height.

Derivative: الْجَوَارِ is derived from the verb جَرَى – يَجْرِي which means to run, to flow, follow a course or in water it can mean to sail or to cruise along. It refers to something that follows a course or sails on the sea, which is a ship or boat. Grammar point: الْجَوَارِ is an inflexible noun (مَمْنوع من الصَّرْف – Mamnoo’ Min As-Sarf) and that is why it has a Kasrah (ِ) at the end instead of a Dammah (ُ).

Derivative: Grammar point: فَانٍ like الْجَوَارِ, فَانٍ is an inflexible noun (مَمْنوع من الصَّرْف – Mamnoo’ Min As-Sarf) and that is why it has a Kasrah (ِ) at the end instead of a Dammah (ُ). فَانٍ is derived from فَنِيَ – فَناءٌ and means “to be meant to die, decease, come to an end and cease to exist”.

Derivative: الْإِكْرَامِ (Al-Ikraam) has a different meaning to كَرَامَة (Karaamah) in Arabic. كَرَامَة (Karaamah) means honour and dignity whilst إِكْرام (Ikraam) means to provide honour and dignity.

Derivative: شَأْنٍ in the Arabic language is another word for أَمْر (Amr) which is an issue or matter. شَأْنٍ refers to something that someone needs to be done but will not be done unless that person does it. It relates to a matter that is specific to the one doing it and cannot be delegated to others.

Derivative: سْأَلُ from سَأَلَ – سُؤَال does not just mean to ask but also implies dependency. The one who is dependent is known as the سائِل (Saa’il), the one who is unable to help himself and asks others.

Derivative: لْإِنْسَانَ (Al-Insaan) “The human being”. There are two argued origins for the word لْإِنْسَانَ (Al-Insaan). There is أُنْس which means compassion, something that a human is both capable and in need of giving and receiving. Such a creation is therefore in need of Ar-Rahman and Ar-Rahman fulfils that through his revelation “Al-Qur’an”. The other argued origin is نَسِيَ (Nasiya) which means to forget, which is an inherent quality of the human being. If someone is forgetful, they are need of a reminder and Al-Qur’an is the ultimate reminder.

خَلَقَ الْإِنْسَانَ

- خَلَقَ الْإِنْسَانَ is the second predicate (Khabar) for the subject (Mubtada’) of الرَّحْمَٰنُ and so it follows that “the incredibly merciful (الرَّحْمَٰنُ) created the human being (خَلَقَ الْإِنْسَانَ)” and that this creation is linked to mercy and an act of mercy.
Derivative: نَفْرُغُ from فَرَغَ which means to free up. نَفْرُغُ means “We will make time” i.e., to deal with you.
Derivative: الثَّقَلَانِ means “two heavy populations” referring to mankind and the Jinn.
Derivative: شُوَاظٌ is a flame.

- وَرْدَةً كَالدِّهَانِ literally means “Red or crimson coloured like oil”. This phrase is used when a lamb is slaughtered and is skinned. Here, the sky on the Day of Judgement is presented by this image, like a helpless creature which Allah peals the skin of.

Derivative: الْبَيَانَ comes from بَانَ – يَبِينُ which specifically means to separate and the word بَيْنَ (between) comes from it, which reflects a chasm or gap between two things. بَيَان (Bayaan) means the ability to separate ideas, chapters, paragraphs, books etc. A complicates issue is solved by breaking it up into separate parts. In this way humans were given the ability to articulate in a manner which is more advanced manner than any other creature and this is بَيَان (Bayaan), the sophisticated ability of communication. In Arabic the words that are related to clarity are related to separation whilst the verbs related to confusion are related to merging and mixing things together. Bayaan applies to both the ability to communicate and to the ability to receive communication. بَيَان (Bayaan) also means clarity i.e., the ability to see with clarity or to clarify things and to deliver clarity to others.
Derivative: يَطُوفُونَ from طَافَ which means to go around or to end up where you started. Here it refers to going back and forth from one place to another.
- حَمِيمٍ is scalding water and آنٍ means a time and now. Together it refers to the scalding water when it reaches the time when its at its extent of heat.

Derivative: مَقَامَ can be a Masdar from أَقَامَ referring to standing or being in a position before “his Rabb”. Or it can be a Zharf (ظَرْف) referring to the time or place of standing before “his Rabb”.

Derivative: حُسْبَانٍ is derived from حِساب which means calculation. حُسْبَانٍ is the hyperbolised form of it, the powerful form, meaning an extreme calculation that is extremely precise. حُسْبَانٍ can also mean a timely destruction or calamity (ref: Surah Kahf: 40).

الشَّمْسُ وَالْقَمَرُ بِحُسْبَانٍ

- According to the two meanings of حُسْبَانٍ (Husbaan), the verse can mean that “The sun and the moon are dependent upon or constrained by precise calculation” and it can mean by combining the meanings that “The sun and the moon are on the verse of a pre-calculated destruction”.
- The addition of بِ before حُسْبَانٍ (calculation) means to depend upon or to be constrained by.
Derivative: إِسْتَبْرَقٍ refers to a thick silk (of the highest quality).
Derivative: بَطَائِنُ refers to that which is in the inside like the insides or inner linings of the cushions, bedding etc.

Derivative: مُدْهَامَّتَانِ – In relation to the two gardens مُدْهَامَّتَانِ refers to a blackness or darkness caused by the lushness of their vegetation, where they are so lush that light doesn’t penetrate.
Derivative: نَضَّاخَتَانِ – نَضَّاخ is in the Mubaalaghah (intensified and more powerful) form and it means to gush or burst out in a powerful manner.
Derivative: حِسَانٌ is the plural of حَسَنٌ and refers to beauty.

Derivative: خَيْرَاتٌ – Is a description for something omitted, which is women. And refers to goodness (خَيْر - Khair) as a descriptive quality and can also refer to choice (خِيرَة). The meaning can be combined to produce the meaning of “being the best of what you want” in terms of qualities and character.
Derivative: مَقْصُورَاتٌ – مَقْصُور means to be restricted, restrained and held back.

Derivative: حُورٌ is reference to a beautiful woman whose eyes are captivating. حُورٌ describes the eye, the white of which is intensely white and the black of which is intensely black, the beauty of which captivates the beholder.

Derivative: عَبْقَرِيٍّ – عبْقَرِي is an attribution to عَبْقَر and in old Arab tales عَبْقَر refers to the land of the Jinn. عَبْقَرِيّ came to be a reference for something exotic and extraordinary that came from far off lands because it was as if that thing came from a completely different world and unique to one’s usual environment.

Derivative: means that which has بَرَكَة (Barakah) in it. It is commonly translated to mean “blessing” however its terminological meaning is “Al-Khair (i.e., that which is good) and an increase in it (i.e., goodness) that is greater than anticipated”.
